message.group-title: Intensive Care and Critical Care Medicine
message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Purpose</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Several studies have reported adopting prone positioning (PP) in non-intubated patients with COVID-19-related hypoxaemic respiratory failure. This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated the impact of PP on oxygenation and clinical outcomes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We searched PubMed, Embase and COVID-19 living systematic review from December1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    2019 to July23
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    2020. We included studies that reported using PP in hypoxaemic, non-intubated adult COVID-19 patients. Primary outcome measure was the weighted mean difference (MD) in oxygenation parameters (PaO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    /FiO
                    <jats:sub>2,</jats:sub>
                    PaO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    or SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    ) pre and post-PP.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Fifteen single arm observational studies reporting PP in 449 patients were included. Substantial heterogeneity was noted in terms of, location within hospital where PP was instituted, respiratory supports, frequency and duration of PP. Significant improvement in oxygenation was reported post-PP: PaO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    /FiO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    , (MD 37.6, 95%CI 18.8, 56.5); PaO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    , (MD 30.4 mmHg, 95%CI 10.9, 49.9); and SpO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    , (MD 5.8%, 95%CI 3.7, 7.9). Patients with a pre-PP PaO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    /FiO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    ≤150 experienced greater oxygenation improvements compared with those with a pre-PP PaO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    /FiO
                    <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                    >150 (MD 40.5, 95%CI −3.5, 84.6) vs. 37, 95%CI 17.1, 56.9). Respiratory rate decreased post-PP (MD −2.9, 95%CI −5.4, −0.4). Overall intubation and mortality rates were 21% (90/426) and 26% (101/390) respectively. No major adverse events were reported.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Despite significant variability in frequency and duration of PP and respiratory supports, PP was associated with improvements in oxygenation parameters without any reported serious adverse events. Major limitation being lack of control arm and adjustment for confounders. Clinical trials are required to determine the effect of awake PP on patient-centred outcomes.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Systematic review registration</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Registration/protocol in PROSPERO (CRD42020194080).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What is the key question?</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Is the novel approach of prone positioning in non-intubated patients associated with improvement in oxygenation?</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>What is the bottom line?</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Prone position in non-intubated severe COIVD 19 suffers is associated with improvement of oxygenation while the short- and long-term patient centred out comes in this awake prone patient need more investigation.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Why read on?</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our study is first of its kind (Systematic review and Meta-analysis) summarising the evidence surrounding the less invasive innovate technique of prone position in non-intubated COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Prone positioning of non-intubated patients with COVID-19 - A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis

reference.unstructured: Counotte M IH , Ipekci M , Low N. COAP Living Evidence on COVID-19, 2020.

GA Wells BS , D O’Connell , J Peterson , V Welch , M Losos , P Tugwell . The Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS) for assessing the quality of nonrandomised studies in meta-analyses Ottawa2013 [Available from: http://www.ohri.ca/programs/clinical_epidemiology/oxford.asp accessed September 4th 2020.
I CIP. COVid-19: Awake Proning and High-flow Nasal Cannula in respiratorY DistrEss (COVAYDE). In: (US) NLoM, ed., 2020.
ClinicalTrialsgov [Internet]. McNicholas B LJ . Awake Prone Positioning to Reduce Invasive VEntilation in COVID-19 Induced Acute Respiratory failurE (APPROVE-CARE). In: (US) NLoM, ed., 2020.
